fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to finish up the metrics library work we've been discussing. basically we need something that lets services track numbers over time — things like totals that only go up, values that can go up or down, and distribution-style tracking. there's also some stuff around making sure the names people give to these metrics are actually valid before we store them — remember we had that bug last quarter where someone passed in a weird string and everything broke downstream? same kind of logic as what we did for the label sanitization in the old collector module.

also we need a way to group related metrics together and spit them out in a format that our scraping infrastructure can actually read. there's a serialization piece here that needs to handle some edge cases with special float values and escaping. and we need an HTTP layer so services can expose their metrics at a path that gets polled.

oh and there's a base64 thing too — some encoded credentials flow through the auth part of the endpoint, so we need to be able to decode those and handle bad input gracefully.

the summary metric type needs to support percentile-like estimates, which i know is the tricky one. just make sure the output matches what the scraper expects. lmk if you have questions, but a lot of this should be obvious from context.

trigger_keywords: what happens if you add a negative value to a counter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A counter must never decrease. If an increment operation is called with a negative amount, the operation is silently ignored and the counter's total remains unchanged. For example: incrementing by 5 then attempting to increment by -5 leaves the counter at 5, not 0.

trigger_keywords: what does decrement with negative value do on gauge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Gauge operations are interpreted arithmetically. Decrementing by a negative value raises the gauge (subtracting a negative is addition). So decrement(-1) results in the gauge going up by 1. Similarly, increment(-1) lowers the gauge by 1.

A histogram always appends a +Inf bucket regardless of the configured finite upper bounds. Even if no buckets are configured, the output must include 'bucket_le=+Inf count=0'. The +Inf bucket count always equals the total sample_count.

When observe_multiple is called and the number of bucket_increments does not match the total number of configured buckets including the +Inf bucket, the operation must be rejected and output: 'error=invalid_bucket_increment_count\nexpected_buckets=N\n' where N is the total bucket count including +Inf. No state is modified.

The 'collect' action on a histogram does NOT reset cumulative counts like sample_count or sample_sum. However, per the test case, the bucket counts for finite buckets appear to reset on collect while +Inf and total counts continue to accumulate all observations. Specifically: after a collect, new observations produce output as if the per-bucket counts restarted from zero but total count and sum accumulate across the whole sequence.

trigger_keywords: what prefix is reserved for metric names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Metric names starting with '__' (double underscore) are reserved and must be rejected as invalid. Label names follow the same rule. Additionally, names must be non-empty and contain only permitted identifier characters (letters, digits, underscores), with no spaces or special characters like '|' or '-'.

trigger_keywords: is dash allowed in label name
what characters are valid in label names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Label names must match the same identifier rules as metric names: only letters, digits, and underscores are allowed. Hyphens/dashes are not permitted. So 'log-level' and '-abcd' are both invalid. A valid label name like 'type' with only alphanumeric and underscore characters is accepted.

trigger_keywords: what happens if constant label key matches per-sample label key
label key conflict between constant and metric labels
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A metric family must reject registration of a metric whose per-sample label keys conflict (overlap) with the family's constant label keys. The output for a conflict case should report the error. Constant labels are shared across all members; per-sample labels are specific to each member.

trigger_keywords: what error when two families have same name different type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If two metric families with the same name but different types are registered in the same registry, the registry must reject the second registration and output: 'error=metric_family_type_conflict\nname=<name>\n'. Same name and same type families are merged in merge mode.

trigger_keywords: how to serialize NaN or Inf in metrics output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Special floating point values must be serialized as: NaN → 'Nan', positive infinity → '+Inf', negative infinity → '-Inf'. These are used in the text exposition format sample lines. Normal numeric values are serialized as standard numbers.

trigger_keywords: how are backslash and newline escaped in label values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the text exposition format, label values must have the following characters escaped: backslash '\' → '\\', newline '\n' → '\n' (literal backslash-n), and double-quote '"' → '\"'. This applies to values inside the curly-brace label sets on sample lines.

trigger_keywords: what is output for bad base64
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When a base64 string is malformed (invalid alphabet characters, incorrect length not divisible by 4, or bad padding like 'A==='), the output must be: 'error=invalid_base64
encoded=<original_input>
'. An empty string is valid and decodes to an empty decoded value: 'decoded=
'.

trigger_keywords: what status code for unregistered path
http metrics path not found behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If an HTTP request is made to a path that has no registered metric registry, the response must have status 404 with content-type 'text/plain; charset=utf-8'. If a registry is registered at that path, status 200 is returned with the metrics body and the same content-type.

trigger_keywords: what happens after removing a registry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a registry is removed from an HTTP endpoint (by index and path), subsequent requests to that path must return 404. If the registry is re-added after removal, requests to that path should return 200 again. Removal is permanent until explicitly re-registered.

trigger_keywords: how are quantile values calculated in summary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The summary uses an epsilon-approximate quantile estimation algorithm (similar to Greenwald-Khanna or the approach used in prometheus-cpp's detail/ckms_quantiles). Given observations [0, 200] with quantile=0.5 and error=0.05, the estimated value is 0. Given [0, 1, 101] with quantile=0.5, the estimate is also 0. The exact output depends on the underlying CKMS implementation, not a simple sort-and-index.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'collect_counter_family', 'has_counter', 'duplicate_labels', 'collect_two_counters', 'merge_same_counter_family', 'reject_type_conflict', 'remove_and_readd'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same kind of logic as what we did for the label sanitization in the old collector module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The name validation rules defined in feature5_name_validation: names must be non-empty, start with a letter or underscore (not double underscore), and contain only [a-zA-Z0-9_] characters. See feature5_name_validation.json for exact test cases and rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature5_name_validation.json for the full rule set.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some encoded credentials flow through the auth part of the endpoint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The HTTP metrics endpoint (feature10) supports optional authentication, and the base64 feature (feature9) is used to decode credentials. The base64 decode must handle standard alphabet, correct padding, and emit 'error=invalid_base64' for malformed input per feature9_base64_decoding.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: spit them out in a format that our scraping infrastructure can actually read
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Prometheus text exposition format described in feature8_text_exposition_format.json: lines starting with '# HELP' and '# TYPE', followed by sample lines with optional label sets in curly braces, with specific escaping rules and special float representations.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the summary metric type needs to support percentile-like estimates, which i know is the tricky one
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The summary (feature4) uses CKMS (Cormode-Korn-Muthukrishnan-Srivastava) quantile estimation with configurable quantile and error parameters. The output format is 'quantile=<q> value=<v>' per configured quantile after 'sample_count' and 'sample_sum' lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory or look at how the related feature was handled in the PRD? If it's about behavior at the boundary, assume we want it to be consistent with whatever the scraper infrastructure expects.

feature10_http_metrics_endpoint.json.description: An HTTP endpoint exposes registered metric registries at configured paths with status, content-type, optional compression, authentication, removal, and expired-registry behavior observable through requests.

cases.expected_output:
request_path=/first
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_first_total=yes
contains_second_total=no
request_path=/second
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_second_total=yes
contains_first_total=no

request_path=/metrics
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
contains_first_total=yes
contains_second_total=no

feature1_counter_accumulation.json.description: Counter samples expose the accumulated non-negative increment total after a sequence of observations.

feature2_gauge_value_updates.json.description: Gauge samples expose the latest numeric value after increments, decrements, and direct assignments.

feature3_histogram_buckets.json.description: Histogram samples expose total count, total sum, and cumulative bucket counts for configured upper bounds plus an infinity bucket.

sample_sum=0
bucket_le=1 count=0
bucket_le=2 count=0
bucket_le=+Inf count=0

bucket_le=2 count=6
bucket_le=+Inf count=7

sample_count=43

bucket_le=2 count=34

sample_sum=-10

bucket_le=+Inf count=1

bucket_le=1 count=0

bucket_le=+Inf count=2

feature4_summary_quantiles.json.description: Summary samples expose total count, total sum, and configured quantile estimates after observations.

feature5_name_validation.json.description: Metric and label names are accepted only when they are non-empty, unreserved, and use the permitted identifier characters.

input.name: fa mi ly with space in name or |

cases.expected_output:
kind=metric
name=fa mi ly with space in name or |
valid=no

feature7_registry_collection_rules.json.description: A registry exposes registered metric families, removes them when requested, merges matching families in merge mode, and rejects name/type conflicts.

feature8_text_exposition_format.json.description: Metric families serialize to the text exposition format with HELP/TYPE headers, escaped label values, special floating-point values, timestamps, and compound sample lines.

cases.expected_output:
# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric Nan

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric -Inf

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric +Inf

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\\v"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\nv"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric gauge
my_metric{k="v\"v"} 0

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric untyped
my_metric 64

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric counter
my_metric 64 1234

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric histogram
my_metric_count 2
my_metric_sum 200
my_metric_bucket{le="1"} 1
my_metric_bucket{le="+Inf"} 2

# HELP my_metric my metric help text
# TYPE my_metric summary
my_metric_count 2
my_metric_sum 200
my_metric{quantile="0.5"} 0

feature9_base64_decoding.json.description: Base64 encoded input decodes to its byte string, while malformed alphabet, size, or padding input yields a neutral invalid-input error.
